ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific term for green milk tea with reduced sugar; 'tea beverage' (FOODON_03301099) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (Tea, milk tea) is the closest defensible match; no specific code exists for green milk tea with less sugar customisation.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept found for green milk tea with reduced sugar; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One large cup (700 g) is the standard serving size for freshly made green milk tea purchased from a Singaporean bubble tea or kopitiam outlet.

nutrient_source.title: Green Milk Tea (Less Sugar) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, iron) sourced directly from HPB Foodo record for Green Milk Tea (Less Sugar), cr_id D08010035, confidence 0.95. Secondary nutrients (cholesterol, saturated fat, trans fat, vitamin C) were present in the input record attributed to a delivery platform source; these have been retained at face value but downgraded to confidence 0.70 and cross-checked for plausibility against USDA FoodData Central entry for tea with milk (FDC ID 174906). Values are consistent with expected ranges for a dilute green tea and milk beverage.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Sweetened Milk Tea — analogous estimate based on 'Tea, with milk and sugar' (GI ~55) from the University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for green milk tea with less sugar. The GI of 55 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for sweetened tea with milk (GI ~55). Per the standard GI classification scale (Atkinson et al., 2008; Jenkins et al.), GI ≤55 is classified as 'low'. The 'less sugar' formulation reduces the sugar load relative to standard milk tea but does not eliminate it (5.92 g sugar/100 g). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving / 100 = 55 × (8.82 × 700 / 100) / 100 = 55 × 61.74 / 100 ≈ 33.96, rounded to 34.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Added sugar content (5.92 g/100 g) in a sugar-sweetened beverage independently raises blood pressure risk per AHA 2018 guidelines, despite low sodium (9 mg/100 g).
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request a truly unsweetened version (zero added sugar) or substitute with plain green tea, as even 'less sugar' variants containing >5 g sugar/100 g in a beverage remain a concern for blood pressure management.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Residual added sugar (5.92 g/100 g) with negligible dietary fibre (0.03 g/100 g) and a low-boundary GI of 55 results in meaningful glycaemic impact at a 700 g serving; classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) per ADA guidelines.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this beverage unless reformulated to zero added sugar, as the 'less sugar' designation does not eliminate the glycaemic risk associated with liquid sugar intake and SSB classification per ADA 2024 Standards of Care.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (1.0 g/100 g) from milk or non-dairy creamer combined with added sugar (5.92 g/100 g) and trans fat (0.04 g/100 g) contribute to cardiovascular risk, particularly at a 700 g serving size.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption of this beverage due to its saturated fat and added sugar content; requesting an unsweetened version with low-fat dairy milk would substantially reduce cardiovascular risk per AHA dietary guidelines.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Added sugar (5.92 g/100 g) and saturated fat from creamer or milk may promote pro-inflammatory pathways, outweighing the diluted green tea polyphenol benefit.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should request unsweetened green tea with low-fat dairy milk to obtain anti-inflammatory polyphenol benefit without the pro-inflammatory sugar and saturated fat load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Green Milk Tea (Less Sugar) is a freshly prepared Singaporean beverage made by combining brewed green tea with milk or non-dairy creamer and a reduced quantity of sugar or sugar syrup. It is commonly sold at bubble tea shops, kopitiams, and hawker stalls across Singapore, typically served cold in a large cup (700 g). The drink has a mild, slightly grassy green tea flavour balanced by the creaminess of milk or creamer, with a lighter sweetness than the standard version. The 'less sugar' designation typically corresponds to approximately 30–50% less sugar than the default formulation, though residual sugar remains at approximately 5.92 g per 100 g. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 51 kcal, 0.24 g protein, 1.77 g fat (of which 1.0 g is saturated), 8.82 g carbohydrate, 5.92 g sugar, 0.03 g dietary fibre, and 9 mg sodium. Despite the reduced sugar label, this beverage is still classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) and carries moderate-to-high concern for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. The green tea component contributes catechins and a small amount of vitamin C (0.71 mg/100 g), but these benefits are substantially diluted in the final beverage. Individuals seeking health benefits from green tea are advised to consume plain unsweetened green tea instead.

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Foodo Singapore database (cr_id D08010035, confidence 0.95). Secondary nutrients (cholesterol, saturated fat, trans fat, vitamin C) retained from input at confidence 0.70 and cross-checked for plausibility against USDA FoodData Central tea-with-milk reference. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for sweetened tea with milk. Overall record confidence is high for the HPB-sourced fields.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this freshly prepared green milk tea formulation are not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. 2. Cholesterol (2.86 mg/100 g), saturated fat (1.0 g/100 g), trans fat (0.04 g/100 g), and vitamin C (0.71 mg/100 g) were present in the input record attributed to a delivery platform source (confidence 0.70); these values have been retained as they are plausible for a milk-containing tea beverage and are consistent with USDA FoodData Central ranges for tea with milk. 3. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for sweetened tea with milk; no direct GI measurement exists for green milk tea with less sugar. Per the standard GI classification scale (Atkinson et al., 2008), GI ≤55 is classified as 'low'; gi_category is therefore set to 'low'. 4. The 'less sugar' designation in Singapore typically corresponds to approximately 30–50% less sugar than the default formulation; the residual sugar of 5.92 g/100 g still classifies this as a sugar-sweetened beverage per ADA and AHA definitions. 5. Portion size of 700 g (1 large cup) is sourced from the input record attributed to a delivery platform; this is consistent with large-cup serving sizes at Singaporean bubble tea outlets.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Corrected ontology match types from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent'.
Iteration 2: Verified alias consistency between aliases_by_language and flat aliases array; added missing 'Teh Susu Hijau (Kurang Gula)' entry.

Iteration 3: Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' to match glycemic_index value of 55 per standard GI classification scale (GI ≤55 = low, per Atkinson et al. 2008). Updated gi_source notes and record notes to document this classification basis explicitly.
